info.title: Docsie Partner API
info.description:
Partner-facing API for provisioning Docsie workspaces, managing content, publishing deployments, tracking AI credit usage, and running asynchronous automation such as exports and video-to-docs.

This reference is intentionally scoped to the authenticated `/api_v2/003/` surface used by partners and embedded product integrations. Public portal, help-center, and legacy deployment endpoints are documented separately.

## How to read this API
- Authenticate every request with `Authorization: Api-Key <partner_api_key>`.
- Most list endpoints use `limit` and `offset` pagination.
- Many object references support `expand[]` so related resources can be returned inline.
- Export, build, and AI workflows return background job records that should be polled through the Jobs APIs.

## Content model
`Workspace -> Documentation Shelf -> Book -> Version -> Language -> Article`

## Recommended integration flow
Start by creating a workspace, then create a documentation shelf, books, versions, and articles. When content is ready, use deployments to publish it and jobs endpoints to monitor asynchronous processing.

get.description:
Return all airgapped builds for your organization.

An **airgapped build** is a self-contained package of your documentation that can be deployed on-premise or in air-gapped environments without internet access. Builds include static HTML, optional Docker images, and Helm charts.

**Build process:** Builds are generated asynchronously. Poll the build status or use webhooks to know when the download is ready.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: Number of results to return per page.

parameters.name: offset
parameters.description: The initial index from which to return the results.

post.description:
Trigger a new airgapped build for a deployment. Requires `deployment_id`.

**Options:**
- `deployment_type`: "portal" (default) or "helpcenter"
- `include_docker`: Include Docker image (default: true)
- `include_helm`: Include Helm chart (default: true)
- `plugins`: List of plugin names to include

Returns immediately with build metadata. Use the `download` action when status is "complete".

tags.name: Workspaces
tags.description:
A workspace is the top-level organizational unit inside a Docsie organization. Every documentation shelf, book, file, deployment, and team member belongs to exactly one workspace.

Use workspaces to separate concerns — for example, one workspace per product line, per team, or per customer. Each workspace has its own member list, file storage, deployment configuration, and optional custom domain.

Workspaces support role-based access: **owners**, **administrators**, **editors**, and **viewers**. You can invite users to a workspace with a specific role using the invite endpoint.

**Related resources:** Documentation shelves belong to a workspace. Use `expand[]=documentation` to inline shelves when listing or retrieving workspaces.

tags.name: Documentation
tags.description:
A documentation shelf (or simply "documentation") is a container that holds one or more books. It represents a single documentation project or product area and carries top-level publishing settings such as password protection, custom styles, and testing hosts.

Shelves sit directly under a workspace in the content hierarchy:
`Workspace → Documentation Shelf → Book → Version → Language → Article`

When you publish content, the deployment references a shelf (or a filtered subset of its books). Shelves can be password-protected and can carry custom branding and style overrides.

**Key operations:** Create shelves, manage passwords, import external content, and export entire shelves in bulk using the export and import endpoints.

tags.name: Books
tags.description:
A book is a collection of related articles within a documentation shelf. Books are the primary structural unit that readers navigate — think of them as sections, guides, or categories within your documentation.

Each book supports **versioning** and **translation**. When you create a book, Docsie automatically creates a default version and a default language. You can then add additional versions (e.g., v1, v2) and languages (e.g., English, French, Japanese) as needed.

Books can be password-protected independently from their parent shelf. You can export a book in multiple formats and languages using the export endpoint.

**Related resources:** A book contains versions, which contain languages, which contain articles. Use `expand[]=versions` to inline version data.

tags.name: Versions
tags.description:
A version represents a release track within a book. Use versions to maintain documentation for different product releases side by side — for example, `v1.0`, `v2.0`, and `latest`.

Each version contains one or more languages, and each language contains the actual article content. When a reader views your documentation, they select both a version and a language.

Versions are ordered and can be marked as active or archived. The `active_languages_count` field tells you how many translations exist for a given version.

**Typical workflow:** Create a version for each product release. Clone articles from a previous version when starting a new release cycle.

tags.name: Languages
tags.description:
A language represents a translated copy of all articles within a specific book version. Languages use ISO 639-1 codes (e.g., `en`, `fr`, `ja`, `de`) and are created per-version.

Docsie supports 100+ languages including right-to-left scripts. You can translate content manually or use the platform's AI-powered translation system, which supports AWS Translate, DeepL, and GPT-4 as translation engines.

Each language contains its own set of articles. When you retrieve a language resource, use `expand[]=articles` to inline the article list.

**Note:** The default language is created automatically when a version is created.

tags.name: Articles
tags.description:
An article is an individual documentation page. Articles use a **block-based content model** — the `doc` field contains structured JSON blocks representing headings, paragraphs, code samples, images, callouts, tables, and other content elements.

Articles belong to a language within a version within a book. They can be published or kept in draft state, and they support ordering within their parent book.

The article content model is designed for rich, structured documentation. You can import content from markdown using the import endpoint, or export articles to markdown and other formats.

**Key fields:**
- `doc` — Block-based content (JSON)
- `template` — Presentation template for rendering
- `published` — Whether the article is visible in deployments
- `order` — Sort position within the book

tags.name: Comments
tags.description:
Comments enable collaboration and review workflows on documentation resources. Team members can leave comments on articles, books, or other content to discuss changes, flag issues, or assign tasks.

Comments support threading via replies, file attachments, and optional due dates for task-oriented feedback. Each comment tracks its author, creation time, and read state.

**Typical use:** Editorial review before publishing, inline feedback from stakeholders, and task assignment during documentation sprints.

tags.name: Deployments
tags.description:
A deployment publishes Docsie content to a customer-facing destination — a documentation portal, help center, or embedded widget. Deployments connect your content to the outside world.

Each deployment references a content source (typically a documentation shelf or a filtered subset of books) and carries configuration for the reader experience: branding, script version, analytics, security settings, and optional custom domain.

Deployments support **advanced security** modes including signed access and domain restrictions. The `master_key` field (returned masked) is used for programmatic access control.

**Key operations:**
- Create and configure deployments
- Refresh a deployment to regenerate cached assets
- Assign a custom domain with SSL via the domain management flow

tags.name: Files
tags.description:
The Files API manages uploaded assets — images, PDFs, videos, and other attachments used within your documentation content.

File uploads use a **two-step flow:**
1. Call `generate_temp_url` to get a presigned upload URL
2. Upload the file binary directly to that URL
3. Call `upload` to register the file in Docsie and get back a file record

Files can be marked as **public** (served via CDN) or **secure** (requires authenticated access). Workspace-level file security settings control the default behavior for new uploads.

Use `presigned_url` to generate time-limited download links for secure files, and `compress_image` to optimize image assets.

tags.name: Jobs
tags.description:
Jobs track asynchronous work created by the platform — exports, imports, builds, AI processing, and other long-running operations.

When you trigger an export, import, or build through another endpoint, the response includes a job record with a `job_id`. Use the Jobs API to poll the job status until it reaches a terminal state (`completed`, `failed`, or `canceled`).

**Job lifecycle:** `pending` → `processing` → `completed` | `failed`

Completed jobs include a `result` field with structured output and, for export jobs, a `download_url` for retrieving the generated artifact.

tags.name: Notifications
tags.description:
Notifications surface workspace activity and system events relevant to the authenticated user. They cover content changes, comments, invitations, deployment updates, and job completions.

Each notification includes an `actor` (who did it), a `verb` (what happened), and a `target` (what was affected). Use `expand[]` to inline actor and target details.

Mark notifications as read using the `read` action to keep the unread count accurate.

tags.name: Search
tags.description:
Search endpoints query the full-text index of documentation content within the authenticated organization. Results are scoped to content the caller has access to.

The search index covers articles, files, and metadata across all workspaces and deployments. Results include relevance scoring and highlight snippets.

tags.name: Query suggestion
tags.description:
Query suggestion endpoints power type-ahead and autocomplete experiences. Send a partial query string and receive ranked suggestions based on indexed documentation content.

Use these endpoints to build search-as-you-type UIs in embedded documentation portals.

tags.name: Snippets
tags.description:
Snippets are reusable content blocks that can be embedded across multiple articles. When a snippet is updated, every article that references it reflects the change.

Use snippets for content that appears in many places — disclaimers, API authentication instructions, shared setup steps, legal notices, or standard warnings.

Snippets use the same block-based content model as articles. The `used_in` field tracks which articles currently reference each snippet.

tags.name: Templates
tags.description:
Templates provide reusable content structures that standardize how new documentation is created. When a user creates a new book or article from a template, the template's structure, default content, and settings are cloned into the new resource.

Use templates to enforce consistency across documentation projects — for example, a standard API reference layout, a release notes template, or a troubleshooting guide structure.

tags.name: Users
tags.description:
The Users API lists and manages members of the authenticated organization. Users can have different roles across workspaces (owner, administrator, editor, viewer).

Use this endpoint to look up user details, check workspace membership, and manage user profiles within the organization context.

tags.name: Credits
tags.description:
AI credits are the unified currency for all AI-powered operations in Docsie — video processing, PDF import, website import, AI chat, RAG search, and machine translation.

Organizations have two credit pools:
- **Monthly credits** — allocated by plan tier, reset each billing cycle
- **Purchased credits** — bought separately, never expire

Use the `balance` endpoint to check available credits before submitting AI workloads, and the `usage` endpoint to review transaction history.

tags.name: Video to Docs
tags.description:
Video to Docs converts source videos into structured documentation with AI. The system extracts key frames, transcribes audio, generates image descriptions, and assembles the output into articles with embedded screenshots.

Four quality tiers are available — each tier controls the sampling rate (frames per second) and the depth of AI analysis, with corresponding credit costs.

**Quality tiers and pricing (AI credits per minute of video):**
| Tier | Credits/min | Detail level |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| `draft` | 250 | Fast overview — good for quick summaries |
| `standard` | 500 | Balanced quality and speed |
| `detailed` | 1,000 | High detail — captures most on-screen changes |
| `ultra` | 2,000 | Maximum detail — every few seconds |

All operations consume **AI credits** from your organization's credit balance. Use the `estimate` endpoint to preview the cost before submitting. Credits are deducted when the job completes. Check your balance via the Credits endpoints.

**Workflow:**
1. Call `estimate` with a video URL or file to preview credit cost
2. Call `submit` to start processing
3. Poll `status` until the job completes
4. Retrieve the generated documentation via `result`

Jobs can be canceled while in progress using the `cancel` endpoint.

tags.name: Compliance
tags.description:
The Compliance API provides AI-powered content screening for video, audio, and text. Submit content against built-in policy presets or freeform rules and receive a detailed violation report with severity levels, time-range locations, and remediation guidance.

**Built-in policy presets:**
- `pii` — Detect personally identifiable information (names, emails, SSNs, etc.)
- `hipaa` — Detect protected health information (patient names, medical records, etc.)
- `brand` — Check for competitor branding, outdated logos, unapproved claims
- `training_qa` — Detect outdated procedures, missing safety warnings, quality issues
- `hate_speech` — Detect hate speech, discriminatory language, harassment

You can also provide freeform policy rules as plain text for custom compliance checks.

**Workflow:**
1. Call `estimate` to preview credit cost
2. Call `submit` with content and policy rules
3. Poll `status` until the analysis completes
4. Review violations with severity levels and time-range locations

**Supported content types:** video (URL or file), audio (file), text (inline or file).

**Pricing (AI credits):**
| Content type | Rate |
|-------------|------|
| Video | 500 credits/min (standard tier) |
| Audio | 250 credits/min |
| Text | 60 credits per 1,000 characters |

All operations consume **AI credits** from your organization's credit balance. Use the `estimate` endpoint to preview the cost before submitting. Credits are deducted when the analysis completes. Check your balance via the Credits endpoints.

tags.name: Airgapped builds
tags.description:
Air-gapped builds package a deployment for offline, on-premise, or restricted-network use. The generated package includes all static assets, reader scripts, and content needed to serve documentation without an internet connection.

Builds can optionally include Docker images and Helm charts for Kubernetes-based on-premise deployments. Use the `download` endpoint to retrieve the completed build artifact.

**Note:** Air-gapped builds are an enterprise feature and require the appropriate plan entitlement.
